memory_text:
The Garden Lily spent most of her career as a florist. 
She had a  backyard kitchen garden she transformed. She pointed out every plant by its proper Latin name, explained how to prune each one correctly, and spent an hour completely at peace among the flowers.

The Recipe
 
Lily loved her kitchen garden and her kitchen smelled like bakeries all the time.
 
One afternoon her granddaughter sat beside her and said "Grandma, how do you make your famous banana bread?" 
 
Lily's  eyes focused and she slowly recited the entire recipe from memory — exact measurements, the order of ingredients, even the tip about not over-mixing the batter. 
 
She loved cooking and baking and talking about it!

The Piano
 
Lily spent most of her life as a music teacher, giving lessons out of her living room for nearly 35 years. Though she no longer recognized the house she had lived in for decades or remembered the names of her closest friends, the moment her grandson sat her down at her old upright piano something shifted. She straightened her back the way she always did, placed her hands on the keys with quiet authority, and began to play. Not stumbling or searching — but playing, fully and beautifully, a Chopin nocturne she had taught hundreds of students over the years. Her eyes closed halfway through. Her foot found the pedal without looking. When she finished she sat quietly for a moment, then turned to her grandson beside her and said "sit down, let me show you the left hand part, you're rushing it." He had never taken a single lesson in his life. He sat down anyway. She taught him for the next hour, correcting his posture, counting the beats out loud, tapping his wrist gently when he tensed up — every inch the teacher she had always been. When they finally stopped she looked around the room slowly, a little confused again, but peaceful. She patted his hand and said "you're going to be just fine." He practiced that left hand part every single day for the rest of the year.
